topic: The performance problem that wasn't: diagnosing a slow dashboard in production
format: Share a specific architectural decision — what the options were, what you chose, and what you've learned 6 months later
post:
4.2 seconds to load the dashboard. Users were complaining. My first instinct was wrong.

I assumed it was the ORM. We use Drizzle with MySQL, so I went straight to query inspection — added logging, checked execution plans, found nothing. Queries were fast. Under 80ms each.

The actual problem: 23 separate queries firing on page load.

Every widget on the HR dashboard — headcount, WPS payroll status, work permit tracking, end-of-service gratuity summaries — was fetching independently. tRPC makes it so easy to wire up endpoints that we never stopped to count the cumulative cost. The code looked clean. Runtime behavior was a mess.

The fix wasn't clever. We batched the critical-path queries into one aggregated loader and deferred the secondary widgets. Load time dropped to 900ms. No caching layer, no infra changes.

What I took from this: tRPC's composability is genuinely useful, and it will quietly punish you for it. You build fast, you ship fast, and then production shows you what your architecture actually costs.

We have 4,000+ automated tests. None of them caught this. Tests don't simulate the shape of real user sessions — that's just a gap you have to accept and work around.

If your dashboard feels fine in dev, profile it in production. Seriously, do it before your users do it for you.
